question:
Let $f(x)$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients.  Define a
sequence $a_0,a_1,\ldots$ of integers such that $a_0=0$ and
$a_{n+1}=f(a_n)$
for all $n\geq 0$.  Prove that if there exists a positive integer $m$ for
which $a_m=0$ then either $a_1=0$ or $a_2=0$.
solution:
Recall that if $f(x)$ is a polynomial with integer coefficients,
then $m-n$ divides $f(m)-f(n)$ for any integers $m$ and $n$. In particular,
if we put $b_n = a_{n+1} - a_n$, then $b_n$ divides $b_{n+1}$ for all $n$.
On the other hand, we are given that $a_0=a_m=0$, which implies that
$a_1=a_{m+1}$ and so $b_0=b_m$. If $b_0=0$, then $a_0=a_1=\cdots=a_m$
and we are done. Otherwise, $|b_0| = |b_1| = |b_2| = \cdots$, so
$b_n = \pm b_0$ for all $n$.

Now $b_0 + \cdots + b_{m-1} = a_m - a_0 = 0$, so half of the integers $b_0,
\dots, b_{m-1}$ are positive and half are negative. In particular, there
exists an integer $0<k<m$ such that $b_{k-1} = -b_k$, which is to say,
$a_{k-1} = a_{k+1}$. From this it follows that $a_n = a_{n+2}$ for all
$n \geq k-1$; in particular, for $m=n$, we have
\[
a_0 = a_m = a_{m+2} = f(f(a_0))
= a_2.
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Let $polyfun(unknownx)$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients.  Define a
sequence $starta,firstav,\ldots$ of integers such that $starta=0$ and
$termnplusone=polyfun(termnval)$
for all $indexn\geq 0$.  Prove that if there exists a positive integer $indexm$ for
which $termmval=0$ then either $firstav=0$ or $seconda=0$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Recall that if $polyfun(unknownx)$ is a polynomial with integer coefficients,
then $indexm-indexn$ divides $polyfun(indexm)-polyfun(indexn)$ for any integers $indexm$ and $indexn$. In particular,
if we put $diffnval = termnplusone - termnval$, then $diffnval$ divides $diffnplusone$ for all $indexn$.
On the other hand, we are given that $starta=termmval=0$, which implies that
$firstav=termmplusone$ and so $diffzero=diffmval$. If $diffzero=0$, then $starta=firstav=\cdots=termmval$
and we are done. Otherwise, $|diffzero| = |difffirst| = |diffsecond| = \cdots$, so
$diffnval = \pm diffzero$ for all $indexn$.

Now $diffzero + \cdots + diffmminusone = termmval - starta = 0$, so half of the integers $diffzero,
\dots, diffmminusone$ are positive and half are negative. In particular, there
exists an integer $0<indexk<indexm$ such that $diffkminusone = -diffkval$, which is to say,
$termkminusone = termkplusone$. From this it follows that $termnval = termnplustwo$ for all
$indexn \geq indexk-1$; in particular, for $indexm=indexn$, we have
\[
starta = termmval = termmplustwo = polyfun(polyfun(starta))
= seconda.
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $rainstorm(radiance)$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients.  Define a
sequence $tangerine,sunflower,\ldots$ of integers such that $tangerine=0$ and
$moonlight=rainstorm(pineapple)$
for all $umbrella\geq 0$.  Prove that if there exists a positive integer $aardwolf$ for
which $crocodile=0$ then either $sunflower=0$ or $buttercup=0$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Recall that if $rainstorm(radiance)$ is a polynomial with integer coefficients,
then $aardwolf-umbrella$ divides $rainstorm(aardwolf)-rainstorm(umbrella)$ for any integers $aardwolf$ and $umbrella$. In particular,
if we put $honeycomb = moonlight - pineapple$, then $honeycomb$ divides $b_{\umbrella+1}$ for all $umbrella$.
On the other hand, we are given that $tangerine=crocodile=0$, which implies that
$sunflower=salmonella$ and so $marzipane=shoelaces$. If $marzipane=0$, then $tangerine=sunflower=\cdots=crocodile$
and we are done. Otherwise, $|marzipane| = |gladiolus| = |peppermint| = \cdots$, so
$honeycomb = \pm marzipane$ for all $umbrella$.

Now $marzipane + \cdots + campfire = crocodile - tangerine = 0$, so half of the integers $marzipane,
\dots, campfire$ are positive and half are negative. In particular, there
exists an integer $0<saxophone<aardwolf$ such that $lullabies = -aardvarks$, which is to say,
$windchime = magnolia$. From this it follows that $pineapple = stalemate$ for all
$umbrella \geq saxophone-1$; in particular, for $aardwolf=umbrella$, we have
\[
tangerine = crocodile = blueberry = rainstorm(rainstorm(tangerine))
= buttercup.
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $constantmap(constantval)$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients.  Define a
sequence $terminalseed,precedentone,\ldots$ of integers such that $terminalseed=0$ and
$a_{n+1}=constantmap(specificelem)$
for all $staticindex\geq 0$.  Prove that if there exists a positive integer $fluidindex$ for
which $randomelem=0$ then either $precedentone=0$ or $antecedenttwo=0$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Recall that if $constantmap(constantval)$ is a polynomial with integer coefficients,
then $fluidindex-staticindex$ divides $constantmap(fluidindex)-constantmap(staticindex)$ for any integers $fluidindex$ and $staticindex$. In particular,
if we put $sumsignal = a_{n+1} - specificelem$, then $sumsignal$ divides $b_{n+1}$ for all $staticindex$.
On the other hand, we are given that $terminalseed=randomelem=0$, which implies that
$precedentone=a_{m+1}$ and so $finalsummand=constantgap$. If $finalsummand=0$, then $terminalseed=precedentone=\cdots=randomelem$
and we are done. Otherwise, $|finalsummand| = |lastsummand| = |edgesummand| = \cdots$, so
$sumsignal = \pm finalsummand$ for all $staticindex$.

Now $finalsummand + \cdots + b_{m-1} = randomelem - terminalseed = 0$, so half of the integers $finalsummand,
\dots, b_{m-1}$ are positive and half are negative. In particular, there
exists an integer $0<rigidindex<fluidindex$ such that $postmeasure = -premeasure$, which is to say,
$afterpiece = priorpiece$. From this it follows that $specificelem = a_{n+2}$ for all
$staticindex \geq rigidindex-1$; in particular, for $fluidindex=staticindex$, we have
\[
terminalseed = randomelem = a_{m+2} = constantmap(constantmap(terminalseed))
= antecedenttwo.
\]

garbled_string.question: Let $abcdezxy(qzxwvtnp)$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients.  Define a sequence $hjgrksla,plmqwzke,\ldots$ of integers such that $hjgrksla=0$ and $zxcvbnma=abcdezxy(sdfghjkl)$ for all $lzmxnvcb\geq 0$.  Prove that if there exists a positive integer $qpowieur$ for which $rtyuiope=0$ then either $plmqwzke=0$ or $vbncxmle=0$.
garbled_string.solution:
Recall that if $abcdezxy(qzxwvtnp)$ is a polynomial with integer coefficients, then $qpowieur-lzmxnvcb$ divides $abcdezxy(qpowieur)-abcdezxy(lzmxnvcb)$ for any integers $qpowieur$ and $lzmxnvcb$. In particular, if we put $cvbnmzas = zxcvbnma - sdfghjkl$, then $cvbnmzas$ divides $cvbnmzas$ for all $lzmxnvcb$. On the other hand, we are given that $hjgrksla=rtyuiope=0$, which implies that $plmqwzke=poiulkjh$ and so $rewqlkas=sodifjwe$. If $rewqlkas=0$, then $hjgrksla=plmqwzke=\cdots=rtyuiope$ and we are done. Otherwise, $|rewqlkas| = |yuiopghj| = |hjketlzx| = \cdots$, so $cvbnmzas = \pm rewqlkas$ for all $lzmxnvcb$.

Now $rewqlkas + \cdots + vmxncqwe = rtyuiope - hjgrksla = 0$, so half of the integers $rewqlkas, \dots, vmxncqwe$ are positive and half are negative. In particular, there exists an integer $0<hnfgtrew<qpowieur$ such that $tijfoqwe = -pweorjlc$, which is to say, $mnbvcxzq = lkjhgfdx$. From this it follows that $sdfghjkl = asdfghqw$ for all $lzmxnvcb \geq hnfgtrew-1$; in particular, for $qpowieur=lzmxnvcb$, we have
\[
hjgrksla = rtyuiope = qwertyop = abcdezxy(abcdezxy(hjgrksla))
= vbncxmle.
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $f(x)\in\mathbb Z[x]$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients and let $q\neq 0$ be a fixed integer.  Construct a sequence $b_0,b_1,\dots$ of integers by
\[ b_0=q, \qquad b_{n+1}=f(b_n) \quad (n\ge 0). \]
Assume that for some positive integer $\ell$ one has $b_{\ell}=q$.  Prove that at least one of the equalities $b_1=q$ or $b_2=q$ holds.
kernel_variant.solution:
Set d_n=b_{n+1}-b_n (n\geq 0).

1. (Divisibility) For any integers s,t we have s-t divides f(s)-f(t) because f has integer coefficients.  Taking s=b_{n+1} and t=b_n gives d_n\mid d_{n+1} for every n\geq 0.

2. (Equality of two successive differences) Since b_0=b_\ell =q, we also have b_1=f(b_0)=f(b_\ell )=b_{\ell +1}, so
   d_0=b_1-b_0=b_{\ell +1}-b_\ell =d_\ell .

3. If d_0=0 then b_1=b_0=q and we are done.  Otherwise |d_0|\neq 0, and the divisibility chain
   d_0\mid d_1\mid \ldots \mid d_\ell =d_0
forces |d_0|=|d_1|=|d_2|=\ldots  .  Hence every d_n is either d_0 or -d_0.

4. The telescoping sum
   d_0+\ldots +d_{\ell -1}=b_\ell -b_0=0
shows that among the first \ell  differences there are as many d_0's as -d_0's.  Consequently there exists an index 1\leq k<\ell  with
   d_{k-1}=-d_k,
 i.e.
   b_{k-1}=b_{k+1}.
Once b_{k-1}=b_{k+1}, applying f to both sides yields b_k=b_{k+2}, and repeating inductively gives the two-periodicity b_n=b_{n+2} for all n\geq k-1.

5. Taking n=\ell  in this eventual periodicity gives
   q=b_\ell =b_{\ell +2}=f(f(b_\ell ))=f(f(q))=b_2.
Thus b_2=q.  Together with the case d_0=0 handled earlier, we obtain the desired conclusion: either b_1=q or b_2=q. \square

core_steps: Use m−n | f(m)−f(n) for integer-coefficient polynomials to get divisibility among successive differences.
Set b_n = a_{n+1}−a_n; then b_n divides b_{n+1}.
Compare a_0 with a_m (=a_0) to obtain b_0 = b_m; conclude either b_0 = 0 (constant sequence) or every |b_n| is the same non-zero value.
Sum of b_0,…,b_{m−1} is 0 ⇒ some adjacent b’s are opposite in sign ⇒ a_{k−1}=a_{k+1}, giving eventual period 2.
Period-2 equality forces a_2 = a_0; since a_0 is the given fixed value, either a_1 = a_0 or a_2 = a_0.

slot1.description: Chosen ‘anchor’ value at which the sequence starts and later returns; everywhere the argument uses 0 only as this anchor.

slot2.description: Index symbol for the first return to the anchor (called m in the statement). Any positive integer label would work.
